speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is 28 of April, 1995. Survivor's name is William Harvey. Interviewer will be Robert Clary. We're in Los Angeles, California, America. The language today will be in English. We're [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: This is April 28, 1995. My name is Robert Clary. I'm the interviewer, and I'm interviewing William Harvey in Los Angeles, California, USA. And it's going to be in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please spell your name and your name at birth, and where you were born and when you were born. And spell the city also, OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. William Harvey, H-A-R-V-E-Y. I was born to be Vilmos Herskovits, H-E-R-S-K-O-V-I-T-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: Vilmos. You'd better spell that, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: V-I-L-M-O-S. And-- [CLEARS THROAT]

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: In [CLEARS THROAT] 20 of May, 1924.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Berehovo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Spell that, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's B-E-R-E-H-O-V-O.

speaker: interviewer
text: What country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, tell me about your childhood, and mostly, to start with, you-- with your parents or grandparents, where they came from.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [CLEARS THROAT] they-- I believe that they came from the Austria-Hungarian monarchy. At that time, I don't know if they were Hungarian or-- it's-- it's hard to tell. My grandparents I had never known, because I was about a year old when my mother's mother died, my grandmother. And my father's parents also died by the time I was born. So I had no knowledge of them, except what I heard from my mother at the time. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And [CLEARS THROAT] it was a nice time Czechoslovakia. [CLEARS THROAT] I enjoyed growing up there. And naturally, that-- that-- naturally that time, before 1918, it was Hungary, Austria-Hungarian monarchy. In 1919, I believe that Russia came in and took it over. But they weren't very successful, so Czechoslovakia came and take-- took over the place. And at that time, in 1919. So in 1924, I was born, so I was born in Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in 1938, Hungary, when Chamberlain went to Europe and cut-- [CLEARS THROAT] and talked to Hitler and cut up some part of the countries, so this part was given back to Hungary again. So in 1938, or '39, I believe Hungary took it over again.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was a difficult time, because soon as they came in, the Hungarians, they seen Jewish people on the street. So they beat them up. You know, that's the way it started out. It wasn't a good start. So-- [CLEARS THROAT] and there was a great deal of anti-Semitism, even from the Hungarians. So it wasn't very pleasant to grow up under that atmosphere and to-- [CLEARS THROAT] and to suffer. Because I-- I believed that its-- in each human being was created equal. But we were treated as a second-- second-class citizen. So it was very difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you-- before we go that far away, in 1938--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --let me ask you where were your parents born? And what did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I said, I-- I had-- have a difficult time to know exactly where they were-- they were born in that part of the country, but it-- they changed so many times. So I don't know if-- if-- I believe it was Austria-Hungarian monarchy at that time, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Both your parent-- mother and father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Both my-- yeah. And my [CLEARS THROAT] father was in the First World War. And he-- he came back very sick. He was a-- a-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] what do you call it now? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A veteran. [LAUGHS] I had a hard time to get that word.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a war veteran, and he was very sick. So I-- I didn't know too much about my father, because most of the time he was in the hospital, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: What business was he in?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in a-- [CLEARS THROAT] they had some wine-- wine business. You know, they had some land, you know, where the wine was growing, grapes. The-- that part of Czechoslovakia was [CLEARS THROAT] very [CLEARS THROAT] fruitful. And one of the best grapes was growing in that part, in Berehovo. But most of the time, it was difficult, because if you had one bad year and the crop was eaten up by the bugs or something, so you didn't have enough money to pay your taxes, you know. So it was kind of difficult, you know. And-- and as I said that most of the time he was sick, so my-- my mother [CLEARS THROAT] took up [CLEARS THROAT] sewing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want some water?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, thank you. Took up some sewing. And [CLEARS THROAT] she-- she was a very talented lady. And she was more like a designer instead of an-- and she was practically the whole [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] head of the family, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you a big family? Many sisters and brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [CLEARS THROAT]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was six of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about them.

speaker: interviewee
text: [CLEARS THROAT] Four girls and two boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to stop. And we are.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, in-- in the rank with your sisters and brother, who were-- were you the first one? The last one? The middle one?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was the last one.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were the last one?

speaker: interviewee
text: The last one.

speaker: interviewer
text: The baby?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I don't think I really want-- was wanted, [LAUGHS] I just came anyway. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My-- my mother was 42 years old, and I don't believe that she would have, you know, bothered to have another child after having five children. But I came anyway, and here I am. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get along with your-- with your sisters and brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we got along very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- tell me--

speaker: interviewee
text: There-- there was a big age different. There was 10 years between me and my sisters. And my brother, I lost him when [CLEARS THROAT] he was about 19 years old. He just graduated from the eighth gymnasium. He was a very good student. I don't know, actually, [CLEARS THROAT] what kind of a illness he had. He was about 11 years old, and he was very athletic. And he was playing football, and somebody kicked his knee. And ever since that, he-- he was constantly sick, you know, with the knee. And my mother took him to every doctor, and-- and she spent awful lot of time to take-- even took him to Germany to some famous professor. And they just couldn't find out what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [CLEARS THROAT] there was time that he couldn't go to school. I mean, he had to-- he-- he was home for about seven months-- or three-- three-- three months of the year he went to school. And when my mother went up to-- [CLEARS THROAT] to the professor to find out how is my son going to do and graduate, he said, with your son, he's so smart that he-- you don't have to worry. If he comes one day to school a year, he'll be able to graduate, you know [CLEARS THROAT]

speaker: interviewee
text: But unfortunately, he died when he was 19. There was two doctor who was taking care of him, and one doctor gave him an injection. It was a horse serum something. That's what they called-- what I remember. And the other doctor didn't know that this doctor had given it to him, and he gave him one. And nine days after that, I believe, he died from that. The-- you know, it was like malpractice they would call it today, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So [CLEARS THROAT] I didn't know too much about my brother, because there was a tremendous age difference. I believe I must have been five years old when he died, you know. So-- and-- and my sisters, [CLEARS THROAT] two of my sisters remained in-- in-- in Berehovo. And the other two went out to Brussels, Belgium.

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my younger sister, who was-- there was two years difference between us-- her name was Helen. And she-- [CLEARS THROAT] my oldest sister, Gizelle, who was already living in Brussels, Belgium-- they finished school in Brussels-- and my youngest sister went to live with my older sister. She felt that maybe have-- have a better opportunity in life in Belgium than it was in-- in Berehovo. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where there a lot of the Jews in Berehovo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I believe that it was-- the population was about, approximately, 20,000, 21,000 people. And among the 21,000, I believe about 9,000 was Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your parent--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a beautiful town, you know. It was a [CLEARS THROAT] fairly prosperous town for people. And, you know, it was like-- they used to call it like a little Paris. People was told nothing compared to go up to Budapest or to Paris to have their clothes made, you know. It was, you know, very-- very-- very interesting town to-- you know, and very interesting people. And-- [CLEARS THROAT] but [CLEARS THROAT] as I said that for some of the people who wasn't that comfortable, you know, it was a difficult life, also.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your parents religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents was-- wasn't-- my-- my father, I seen him used to go to the temple, you know. Practically every day, and so I would call it that he was religious, you know. And my mother observed all the Jewish tradition and the holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the holidays.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [CLEARS THROAT] We always looked forward to the holidays, you know. It was something special, particularly Pesach when it came, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: [CLEARS THROAT] It was-- it was very, very lovely, you know, when-- when-- when my mother made very special dinner for Pesach. And she was an [CLEARS THROAT] excellent cook, you know. She was an [CLEARS THROAT] extremely good-- matter of fact, you would her today a gourmet cook. When people used to pass by the house, the-- our home, and the window was open and she would cook, she says, Mrs. Herskovits, we have to come in, because the smell what you-- comes out of your kitchen is not an average kitchen smell. We wanted to see what you're doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- [SOBS] [SOBS] I'm sorry. [SOBS] [SOBS] Say something. I shouldn't do that. It's OK. It's OK. Well, I want to ask you about-- did you do your bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: After that many years, I shouldn't be like that. Yes, yes. I was bar mitzvahed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was very nice. Very nice. I had a godfather who insisted that I should become bar mitzvahed, and she was-- he was a very well-respected Jewish man in the city. And they didn't have children, so they practically took to me like their own child, you know. And they treated me like that. And they-- they insisted that they're going to make my bar mitzvah. [CLEARS THROAT] And they-- they did make it, you know. They made the bar mitzvah. He was the president of the temple, you know. And I had a tough time to study up to the bar mitzvah, but I did it, out of his respect.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and they made me very lovely bar mitzvah. And I was able to learn the program, what do you have to go. And particularly, they-- why they were so persistent, because I happened to be a-- what do you call it? A Cohen?

speaker: interviewer
text: A what?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Cohen?

speaker: interviewer
text: A Cohen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cohen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Cohen. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Such a good Jew. [LAUGHTER] Yeah, so.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your-- your-- your schooling? Were you a good student?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was just fair. I wasn't a straight A student, by far.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you enjoy going to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I enjoyed to go to school, yeah. Yep.

speaker: interviewer
text: Here. Have some Kleenex.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't think I'd go and need.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any friends while you were at school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I had a lot of friends. Yeah. I liked-- always liked people. And people seemed to like me, you know. I had no difficulties to make friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you into sports? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I-- I-- I used to love swimming and-- and skiing and ice skating. That was my favorite. And at that time, the only sport what they played in Europe was the kick ball or the football. What they call it here?

speaker: interviewer
text: Soccer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Soccer, they call it here. Yeah. Since I've been here, I haven't been, you know, into it, though I should really get into some sport, because my grandson is-- he-- he plays baseball and he plays basketball. And he's very good at it, too. So I should really learn, because I feel kind of dumb when I go there and I don't understand the game.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you close with your sisters when you a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we were-- particularly to one sister. As I said, two of them, you know, weren't living in Beregszasz-- Berehovo, Beregszasz. And they were in Brussels, Belgium. And then the war came, you know, and Hitler occupied Belgium sooner than Czechoslovakia. They went in there sooner, so we couldn't correspond with them. And we couldn't travel to go to see them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one sister, Margaret, who was staying with my mother-- and she also, you know, did some sewing with my mother together. And they were practically the sole supporter of the family at that time, you know. So I was very close to my sister, Margaret.

speaker: interviewee
text: She-- she was-- you know, she's living here in Los Angeles right now. Unfortunately, she's 81 years old, and she has hardening of the artery, you know. So she's not well, [INAUDIBLE], you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far did you go with your education?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I-- I had gymnasium just before graduation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Gymnasium is like what? The--

speaker: interviewee
text: Like-- like I think it's equivalent to three years of college.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's like college?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's like college, yeah. The-- yeah. Yeah. You have the high school. You have either choice to go to high school or to go to eighth gymnasium.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why-- why did you stop going to the gymnasium?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was-- I didn't stop, actually, before graduation. That-- that was already when Hitler came in to Berehovo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then tell-- tell me about the occupation and what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, when Hitler came in, naturally, the first day they came in, you know, everything changed. Not-- not that it was such a pleasant circumstances under the Hungarian regime either, you know. But when Hitler come-- took over, everything had changed, you know. We were afraid to go on the street. We were afraid to talk to anybody. And they [CLEARS THROAT] beat people up on the street. And they picked up a lot of men, and they took and beat them. I don't know where. And they beat-- beat them. Matter of fact, my father wasn't a well person, and he was beaten up by the Germans. And not too long after that, in 1943, then he died. That was just before they took us away. So he was buried in Beregszasz, Berehovo.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was buried where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Berehovo, Beregszasz. That was in-- under the German occupation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- there were any rules in-- in the city when you were under the occupation? Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: What kind of a rules?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Germans give you rules for the Jews? Did-- any rules?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell-- tell me about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, we weren't able-- we weren't allowed to go outside. We weren't allowed to just freely, you know, go around the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean there were curfews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there was-- there were curfews for the Jews. Only for the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that affect you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was terrible. I mean, I was always an idealistic child, and I always believed on everybody's freedom. And it was very difficult to understand why, just because being Jewish, you have to endure all that kind of a treatment from-- from people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have non-Jewish friends when you were a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they react about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I have to admit that I wish I could, you know, say that there was-- they were very supportive, but they weren't. They turned their cheek on the other way. They weren't very nice, most of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ask them why?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no answer, if I would ask them why, you know. I certainly did, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened to your life then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- it was very, very bad. We were constantly living in fear what going to happen, you know. And even that we heard from some people that they're going to take us to Germany to work and-- and to the camps. We had absolutely no understanding what that really truly meant, you know, about what our future going to be. It didn't look good, but we didn't believe that, in the 20th century, when-- when the human race supposed to be on-- on on the top, far as intelligent concern, and to-- or-- and still to have such a brutal treatment toward another human being. That it's possible to be, you know, in the 20th century.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to wear a yellow Star of David?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yellow stars.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: On-- on-- on my chest.

speaker: interviewer
text: They forced you to do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. You couldn't walk around without it. But we-- [SIGHS] we tried to avoid to do that, you know, to wear it. So we-- we tried to avoid to go down the street. We were afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: [CLEARS THROAT] I-- [CLEARS THROAT] as I said that when-- when the Germans came in, then I was just about ready to graduate. And-- but everything stopped, you know. Couldn't go to school. There was just a certain percentage was allowed to go. I believe it around 6%.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I just stopped. And [CLEARS THROAT] we thought that we're going to emigrate somewhere, and if it's a possibility, that we can go out. So I took a course. They had several different courses you can take, you know. And I took cosmetology up. I thought, you know, that I-- if I go somewhere, somebody suggested to me that it would be a good field to be in if you emigrate to some other countries.

speaker: interviewer
text: What countries you wanted to emigrate?

speaker: interviewee
text: I always had the dream to come to America, because my mother was here-- and she used to talk about it-- in the 1800's and the-- and the early 1900s. She lived in-- with my grandmother-- in New York. And she went to school there. And she always-- you know, she spoke a fairly good English. And [CLEARS THROAT] she always used to talk about America. And her-- all her brothers and sisters remained in America. My uncle lived to be 94. He died in Los Angeles here. And her sister died-- my mother's sister died in San Diego. She also lived in New York. And also, my father's family was all living in America. Only one brother was living in Europe, [INAUDIBLE]. My mother had only one sister. The rest of her family-- she had two sisters living in New York, and a brother was living in Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother ever tell you why they went-- she went back to Berehovo?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. She just-- she just always liked-- she liked Europe. It was-- I suppose it was a little bit difficult-- at that time, in those years, it was a little bit difficult, even in America. But I am sure that [CLEARS THROAT] it would have been far better if she would have remained here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Prior of the Germans occupying your city, have you-- did you ever hear about what was happening in Germany in the '30s?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we were aware of it. We heard-- because my mother had a very close friend of her who was living in Berlin. And, matter of fact, she had sent-- when the anti-Semitism start-- started in Germany and they start to persecute the Jews, her son came to visit us and stayed with us for quite a while, you know. And I don't-- I don't even believe that he went back to Germany. He emigrated someplace else.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that affect you, as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was terrible. I remember it real well. I don't know what year it was when it came to power-- Hitler came to power.

speaker: interviewee
text: '33? And I heard that on the radio, or [INAUDIBLE]. I-- I was a young fellow at the time. And I-- I heard him say, you know, that I'm going to try to kill every Jews I possibly can. And the one who will survive, I make it sure that they're going to lose some member of their family, that they never be happy in life.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were in your teens at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That's-- it-- in '33, so I must have been-- I was born in-- about 9 years old, 10 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of language you spoke at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: We spoke either a little bit of Czech and-- and Hungarian, because it was-- you know, my late years in school was in a Hungarian school.

speaker: interviewer
text: No Yiddish?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- my-- my mother and my father spoke a good Yiddish. I didn't speak it. Just a few word. I learned my Yiddish in-- in a concentration camp, actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't want to rush you, but that's why I want to still go back to-- to your family, you know. How did you-- sixth sister who stayed in Berehovo, reacted about the whole thing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I said, my-- three of my sisters wasn't living at home. They were living in Brussels, Belgium.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the one who was living with you at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: The one who was-- well, it-- it-- you know, it was very difficult for everybody. I mean, it was-- it was very hard, you know. Life wasn't-- was rough, you know. To make a living, it was rough for-- for Jewish people. And to constantly hear that 'you dirty Jews' anywhere you went. You know, and [CLEARS THROAT] when-- naturally when Hungarian took over that part of the country, and-- [CLEARS THROAT] I must have been about 13 years old-- and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all the Hungarian 13 years old supposed to be trained for pre-army. They used to call that levente, whatever that is. I don't even know. [LAUGHS] I don't know what that mean. But anyway, you were--

speaker: interviewer
text: At 13?

speaker: interviewee
text: At-- at 12.

speaker: interviewer
text: At 12?

speaker: interviewee
text: At 11 to 12, they were training you for the army by-- like, after school, you have to go and march. And [CLEARS THROAT] you had a wooden rifle, you know. Not-- not the a one. But [CLEARS THROAT] so the Jewish-- Jewish boys wasn't doing the same thing. They didn't allow to do the Jewish boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to hold it up for the next tape, OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.
